canonical_summary:
ITAAC closure is the regulatory bridge between construction and operation of any Part 52 combined license (COL) nuclear plant in the United States. Under 10 CFR 52.103(g), the Nuclear Regulatory Commission must find that every Inspection, Test, Analysis, and Acceptance Criterion (ITAAC) in the COL has been satisfied before the licensee may load fuel. The mechanics live in Section 52.99: the licensee files an ITAAC Closure Notification (ICN) for each completed ITAAC under 52.99(c)(1), an ITAAC Post-Closure Notification (IPCN) under 52.99(c)(2) if new information surfaces, and the NRC verifies through the Construction Inspection Program under 52.99(d).

The hearing right unique to the operate phase sits in 10 CFR 52.103(b). When the licensee files the 225-day fuel-load notice under 52.103(a), the public has a narrow window to petition for a hearing on whether the as-built plant conforms to the ITAAC, subject to a prima facie showing of nonconformance plus safety significance.

Vogtle Units 3 (398 ITAAC, finding August 3, 2022) and 4 (364 ITAAC, finding July 28, 2023) are the only US precedents for a completed 52.103(g) finding and remain the canonical reference cases for any future AP1000, AP300, or other Part 52 COL holder approaching fuel load.

citations.label: Federal Register, 2016 final 52.103(b) hearing procedures (81 FR 43266)
NUREG-2124, Vogtle 3 and 4 Final Safety Evaluation Report

faqs.question: What is the 10 CFR 52.103(g) finding?
faqs.answer: The 10 CFR 52.103(g) finding is the binary, plant-wide determination by which the Nuclear Regulatory Commission concludes that every acceptance criterion in a combined license has been met, except those already resolved at COL issuance under 52.97(a)(2). Until the Commission makes this finding, the licensee is legally barred from operating the facility, including initial fuel load. The finding is made by the Commission on the staff's recommendation and rests on the staff's review of every 52.99(c) notification plus independent Construction Inspection Program verification under 52.99(d). The forty-year COL term runs from the date of the finding.

faqs.question: When must an ITAAC Closure Notification (ICN) be submitted?
faqs.answer: Under 10 CFR 52.99(c)(1), the licensee must notify the NRC for each ITAAC once the prescribed inspections, tests, and analyses have been performed and the acceptance criteria are met. To support a timely 52.103(g) finding, the ICN must be filed in sufficient time for staff review and Commission action before scheduled fuel load. In practice the operative cutoff is 225 days before scheduled initial fuel loading, the same date that opens the 52.103(a) hearing-petition window. Any ITAAC still uncompleted at that point must be addressed through an Uncompleted ITAAC Notification under 52.99(c)(3).

faqs.question: How many ITAAC did Vogtle Units 3 and 4 have?
faqs.answer: Vogtle Unit 3 had 398 ITAAC associated with combined license NPF-91, and Vogtle Unit 4 had 364 ITAAC associated with combined license NPF-92. The lower Unit 4 count reflects shared site-wide ITAAC, including items tied to the common Turbine Building, switchyard interfaces, and certain emergency planning elements that were closed once during the Unit 3 process and did not require separate closure for Unit 4. Both unit ITAAC sets were closed using ICNs under 10 CFR 52.99(c)(1), verified through the Construction Inspection Program under Inspection Manual Chapter 2503 and Chapter 2504.

faqs.question: When did the NRC make the 52.103(g) finding for Vogtle 3 and Vogtle 4?
faqs.answer: The Nuclear Regulatory Commission made the 10 CFR 52.103(g) finding for Vogtle Unit 3 on August 3, 2022, documented in ADAMS Accession No. ML22215A132 and announced in NRC News Release 22-049. The Commission made the corresponding finding for Vogtle Unit 4 on July 28, 2023, announced in NRC News Release 23-052 and noticed in the Federal Register on August 2, 2023. These are the only two 52.103(g) findings the NRC has issued for any commercial reactor, and they remain the canonical precedents for every future Part 52 combined license holder approaching fuel load.

faqs.question: What does Section 52.99(c)(1) require an ICN to contain?
faqs.answer: 10 CFR 52.99(c)(1) requires the ITAAC Closure Notification to contain sufficient information to demonstrate that the prescribed inspections, tests, and analyses have been performed and that the prescribed acceptance criteria are met. The NRC's endorsed methodology, Regulatory Guide 1.215 Revision 2 and NEI 08-01 Revision 5 Corrected, operationalizes that test as a docketed letter containing the verbatim ITAAC text, a narrative ITAAC closure basis showing methodology and results, an explicit comparison of results to each acceptance criterion, and a signed statement of completion mirroring the regulatory language. Underlying records stay on site for inspector review.

faqs.question: What is an ITAAC Post-Closure Notification (IPCN)?
faqs.answer: Under 10 CFR 52.99(c)(2), an ITAAC Post-Closure Notification is required when new information materially alters the basis for a previously submitted ICN. The IPCN must describe the new information, its impact on the original closure basis, how the licensee resolved the issue, and a re-affirmation that the ITAAC remains satisfied. Regulatory Guide 1.215 directs IPCNs to follow the same format and sufficient-information standard as the original ICN. The IPCN is the regulatory hook that lets the NRC reopen a closed ITAAC if construction realities drift away from the closure basis before the 52.103(g) finding.

faqs.question: Who can request a hearing under 10 CFR 52.103(b)?
faqs.answer: Any person whose interest may be affected by operation of the plant may petition for a hearing under 10 CFR 52.103(b), with standing decided under the general intervention rule 10 CFR 2.309. Petitioners must satisfy ordinary 2.309(d) standing factors, typically through the 50-mile proximity presumption or by showing particularized injury, and must meet the 2.309(f) contention-admissibility requirements. In practice intervenors have been local residents, states and tribes within the emergency planning zone, and public-interest organizations. The window opens with the 52.103(a) notice 225 days before scheduled fuel load and runs for 60 days.

faqs.question: What prima facie showing does Section 52.103(b) require?
faqs.answer: 10 CFR 52.103(b) requires the hearing petitioner to show, prima facie, that one or more acceptance criteria in the COL ITAAC have not been or will not be met, and that the specific operational consequences of nonconformance would be contrary to providing reasonable assurance of adequate protection of public health and safety. Both prongs are mandatory. A petition alleging procedural irregularities, or nonconformance with criteria the licensee can readily cure, fails the safety-significance prong even if the factual nonconformance prong is colorable. The 2016 final hearing procedures at 81 Fed. Reg. 43,266 govern how the Commission evaluates the showing.

faqs.question: Does Part 52 still require ITAAC after the 52.103(g) finding?
faqs.answer: No. Under 10 CFR 52.103(h), once the Commission has made the 52.103(g) finding, the ITAAC do not, by virtue of their inclusion in the combined license, continue as regulatory requirements for either the operating licensee or for renewal of the license. ITAAC for which the Commission granted a hearing under 52.103(a) expire upon final Commission action in that proceeding. The closed ITAAC become a historical record rather than continuing license conditions, and the underlying design, configuration, and operational controls migrate to the technical specifications and other operating-phase requirements.

faqs.question: Can a plant operate while a 52.103(b) hearing is pending?
faqs.answer: Yes, under specific conditions. 10 CFR 52.103(c) directs the Commission, on granting a hearing petition, to consider whether during a period of interim operation there will be reasonable assurance of adequate protection to public health and safety. If so, the Commission may permit interim operation under the COL while the hearing proceeds, so a granted petition does not automatically halt fuel load. Section 52.103(e) further directs the Commission, to the maximum extent possible, to decide the issues within 180 days of the 52.103(a) notice or by the anticipated fuel-load date, whichever is later, to avoid open-ended schedule slip at the threshold of operation.

faqs.question: What NRC inspection guidance governs ICN verification?
faqs.answer: Two Construction Inspection Program documents govern verification. Inspection Manual Chapter 2503 covers inspections of ITAAC-related work and ICN review, establishing how Region II inspectors and headquarters reviewers sample construction, testing, and analysis activities in real time, then check ICN narratives against underlying records and as-built configuration. Inspection Manual Chapter 2504 covers the broader Construction Inspection Program structure under which 2503 operates. Together they implement the staff's 10 CFR 52.99(d) duty to ensure that the prescribed inspections, tests, and analyses are in fact performed, which is the staff's principal basis for recommending the 52.103(g) finding.

faqs.question: How are AI agents being used in ITAAC closure work?
faqs.answer: AI agents are starting to do the document-heavy work that previously bottlenecked ICN production: traceability mapping from design commitments to as-built records, automated drafting of ITAAC closure basis narratives in the NEI 08-01 format, regression checks when design changes or nonconformance reports might trigger an IPCN, and reviewer-facing search across ADAMS, Inspection Manual Chapters, and NUREG-series sources. The closure determination itself remains a licensed-engineer judgment under 10 CFR 52.99(c)(1), but the surrounding evidence assembly, cross-referencing, and quality-assurance checks are increasingly agent-assisted, which matters for new Part 52 COL holders facing hundreds of ITAAC.

h1: ITAAC closure and the 10 CFR 52.103(g) finding: the US Part 52 path to fuel load
meta_title: ITAAC closure and the 10 CFR 52.103(g) finding
meta_description: How ITAAC closure works under 10 CFR Part 52: ICNs, IPCNs, the 52.103(g) all-complete finding, 52.103(b) hearings, and the Vogtle 3 and 4 precedent.

sections.heading: ITAAC and the 10 CFR 52.97 structure
paragraphs: Subpart C of 10 CFR Part 52 governs the back end of a combined license (COL), the integrated construction-and-operation license created by the 1989 Part 52 rule and overhauled in 2007. The COL is issued under 10 CFR 52.97. At issuance, the Commission identifies under 52.97(a)(2) those acceptance criteria already found to have been met in the COL proceeding itself; everything else carries forward as an Inspection, Test, Analysis, and Acceptance Criterion (ITAAC) that the licensee must demonstrate as completed before operation.

An ITAAC is a structured three-part commitment in the COL: a Design Commitment that the plant must meet, the Inspections, Tests, or Analyses that will be performed to verify it, and the Acceptance Criteria that those activities must satisfy. ITAAC are inherited from the underlying design certification (for AP1000 plants, 10 CFR Part 52 Appendix D) and supplemented by plant-specific ITAAC tied to site characteristics, the Final Safety Analysis Report, and emergency planning. Together they form the binary checklist that the Commission must clear under 52.103(g) before the licensee may load fuel.

subsections.heading: Why the structure exists
paragraphs: The Part 52 design is meant to resolve safety questions before construction, then verify execution. Once the COL is issued, the safety conversation is governed by the as-built record against fixed acceptance criteria rather than relitigated design choices. The 52.97(a)(2) carve-out keeps already-resolved questions off the table, and the ITAAC become the operational gate.

sections.heading: The closure process under 10 CFR 52.99
paragraphs: Section 52.99 structures every notification and inspection between COL issuance and the 52.103(g) finding. Section 52.99(a) requires the COL holder to file an ITAAC completion schedule with the NRC no later than one year after COL issuance or the start of construction as defined in 10 CFR 50.10(a), whichever is later. Schedule updates run every six months, then escalate to every thirty days inside the final year before scheduled initial fuel loading, and continue until the last notification is filed.

Section 52.99(c) creates the four notification products. Section 52.99(c)(1) covers the ITAAC Closure Notification (ICN), the affirmative statement that each ITAAC has been satisfied. Section 52.99(c)(2) covers the ITAAC Post-Closure Notification (IPCN), filed when new information materially alters the basis for a previously closed ITAAC. Section 52.99(c)(3) covers the Uncompleted ITAAC Notification (UIN), due no later than 225 days before scheduled fuel load for any ITAAC not yet closed. Section 52.99(c)(4) covers the final all-ITAAC-complete notification, which triggers the Commission's 52.103(g) finding.

Section 52.99(d) is the NRC's independent verification authority: the staff must ensure that the prescribed inspections, tests, and analyses in each ITAAC are in fact performed. The Construction Inspection Program implements that duty through Inspection Manual Chapters 2503 and 2504. Section 52.99(e) handles uncompleted ITAAC: the licensee cannot operate the affected portion of the facility until the Commission has found that ITAAC met.
bullets: Schedule under 52.99(a): one-year initial filing, 6-month updates, 30-day updates in the final year
ICN under 52.99(c)(1): affirmative closure statement per ITAAC
IPCN under 52.99(c)(2): post-closure update when new information surfaces
UIN under 52.99(c)(3): 225 days before fuel load for any open ITAAC
Staff verification under 52.99(d) via the Construction Inspection Program

sections.heading: ICN format and submission under 52.99(c)(1)
paragraphs: 10 CFR 52.99(c)(1) requires each ICN to contain sufficient information to demonstrate that the prescribed inspections, tests, and analyses have been performed and that the prescribed acceptance criteria are met. The NRC's endorsed methodology is Regulatory Guide 1.215, Revision 2, which incorporates by reference NEI 08-01, Revision 5 Corrected. Together they establish the docketed letter as the standard ICN vehicle, placed in ADAMS so it is publicly available, which is what makes the 52.103(b) hearing opportunity meaningful.

Each ICN, per the NEI 08-01 templates, includes a header identifying the COL holder, unit, ITAAC number, and the design certification or COL section in which the ITAAC text appears; a verbatim restatement of the ITAAC table entry (Design Commitment, Inspections/Tests/Analyses, Acceptance Criteria); a narrative ITAAC Closure Basis describing how each activity was performed, the methodology used, and how the results show each Acceptance Criterion is met; and a signed statement of completion mirroring the regulatory language. ICNs for families of identical equipment may use sampling under a first-of-a-kind / nth-of-a-kind treatment.

Section 52.99(c)(1) does not require licensees to docket the underlying inspection reports, test data, calculations, vendor certifications, or as-built drawings with each ICN. Those records stay on site under the COL holder's records-retention and QA programs, available to NRC inspectors during Construction Inspection Program activities governed by Inspection Manual Chapter 2503.

sections.heading: The 10 CFR 52.103(g) all-complete finding
paragraphs: 10 CFR 52.103(g) is the operational linchpin: the licensee shall not operate the facility until the Commission has found that the acceptance criteria in the combined license are met, except for those acceptance criteria that the Commission already found met when it issued the COL under 52.97(a)(2). The finding is binary across the full ITAAC set: every open ITAAC must be closed and accepted, and every IPCN must be resolved, before the finding can issue.

The finding is made by the Commission on the staff's recommendation. Its principal evidentiary basis is the staff's review of every 52.99(c) notification together with the staff's independent ITAAC inspections under 52.99(d). The finding is also a timestamp: under 52.103(h), once the Commission makes the 52.103(g) finding, ITAAC do not, by virtue of their inclusion in the COL, continue as regulatory requirements for either the operating licensee or for renewal of the license. The forty-year COL term runs from the date of the finding, making it the operative date for license-renewal eligibility under 10 CFR Part 54.

sections.heading: The 10 CFR 52.103(b) contention process
paragraphs: 10 CFR 52.103(a) requires the COL holder to notify the NRC of the scheduled date for initial fuel loading not later than 225 days before that date, and to update the notice if the date moves. That notice opens a 60-day window for any person whose interest may be affected by operation of the plant to petition for a hearing on whether the as-built plant will conform to the COL acceptance criteria. The Commission decides whether to grant or deny under 10 CFR 2.309, applying the standard intervention standing and contention-admissibility rules.

10 CFR 52.103(b) layers an additional, ITAAC-specific prima facie burden on top of ordinary 2.309 rules. The petition must show, prima facie, that one or more acceptance criteria in the COL ITAAC have not been or will not be met, and that the specific operational consequences of nonconformance would be contrary to providing reasonable assurance of adequate protection of public health and safety. Both prongs are required. A petition alleging procedural irregularities, or nonconformance with criteria the licensee can readily cure, fails the safety-significance prong even if the nonconformance prong is colorable.

If the Commission grants a hearing, Section 52.103(c) directs it to consider whether interim operation during the hearing will provide reasonable assurance of adequate protection; if yes, the Commission may permit interim operation under the COL while the proceeding continues. Section 52.103(e) directs the Commission, to the maximum possible extent, to decide the issues within 180 days of the 52.103(a) notice or by the anticipated fuel-load date, whichever is later. The 2016 final procedures at 81 Fed. Reg. 43,266 (July 1, 2016) govern the mechanics.

sections.heading: Vogtle precedent: the only completed 52.103(g) findings
paragraphs: Vogtle Electric Generating Plant Unit 3, an AP1000 reactor licensed to Southern Nuclear Operating Company under combined license NPF-91 (issued February 10, 2012), had 398 ITAAC. On August 3, 2022, the NRC made the 10 CFR 52.103(g) finding for Unit 3, the first such finding for any commercial reactor under Part 52. The finding letter is documented in ADAMS Accession No. ML22215A132 and was announced in NRC News Release 22-049.

Vogtle Unit 4, under combined license NPF-92, had 364 ITAAC. The lower count relative to Unit 3 reflects shared site-wide ITAAC closed once during the Unit 3 process: items tied to the common Turbine Building, switchyard interfaces, and certain site-wide emergency planning elements did not require separate Unit 4 closure. On July 28, 2023, the NRC made the 52.103(g) finding for Unit 4, announced in NRC News Release 23-052 and noticed in the Federal Register on August 2, 2023.

The technical backbone of both findings rests on NUREG-1793 (the AP1000 design certification FSER), NUREG-2124 (the Vogtle 3 and 4 plant-specific FSER), and NUREG-0800 (the Standard Review Plan). Both findings remain the canonical reference cases for any future AP1000, AP300, or other Part 52 COL holder approaching fuel load, and the lessons-learned record (including SECY-22-0086 and the AP1000 Construction Inspection Program lessons-learned report) is the operative starting point for the next licensee.

sections.heading: What AI agents are doing in ITAAC closure work
paragraphs: The Vogtle experience showed that ITAAC closure is a document-assembly bottleneck as much as an engineering exercise. Each of the 398 Unit 3 ICNs traced from a design commitment in the AP1000 DCD through inspection procedures, test results, as-built reconciliations, vendor certifications, nonconformance dispositions, and configuration management records to a docketed narrative under NEI 08-01. Multiply that by a fleet of new Part 52 COL holders and the burden compounds.

AI agents are now doing the surrounding work that a licensed engineer's closure determination still rests on: traceability mapping from each ITAAC's Design Commitment to the underlying records, automated drafting of the ITAAC Closure Basis narrative in the RG 1.215 / NEI 08-01 format, regression checks against the corrective-action and configuration-management programs to flag IPCN triggers under 52.99(c)(2), and reviewer-facing search across ADAMS, the Inspection Manual Chapters, and the NUREG-series sources that the staff will use during 52.99(d) inspections.

The closure determination itself remains a 10 CFR 52.99(c)(1) judgment by an authorized licensee representative, and the staff's independent inspection authority under 52.99(d) is untouched. What changes is the cost and cycle time of producing an ICN that a reviewer can verify against the underlying records on first read. For the next Part 52 COL holder, that delta is the difference between a closure campaign that tracks the construction critical path and one that drives it.
